facts.text: Astoria began as Hallet's Cove, a 1652 settlement that later became the namesake of New York’s richest man, John Jacob Astor.
The neighborhood’s historic core includes the National Register sites of Bohemian Hall, the Astoria Center of Israel, and the Steinway Mansion.
Steinway & Sons moved its factory to Astoria in the 1870s, creating a model town with worker housing, a church, and a streetcar line.
Astoria’s waterfront park, once a reclaimed landfill, now hosts the world‑class Socrates Sculpture Park, a testament to the area’s creative resilience.
The WPA‑era pool on Astoria’s waterfront opened in 1936 and hosted Olympic trials, concerts, and countless summer afternoons.
Astoria’s film heritage shines at the Museum of the Moving Image, where the neighborhood’s role in Hollywood history is celebrated.
